text: What is the form of government of the United States?
answer: Republic (or Constitution-based federal republic; representative democracy)

text: What is the supreme law of the land?
answer: (U.S.) Constitution

text: Name one thing the U.S. Constitution does.
answer: Forms the government (defines powers of government; defines the parts of government; protects the rights of the people)

text: The U.S. Constitution starts with the words “We the People.” What does “We the People” mean?
answer: Self-government (popular sovereignty; consent of the governed; people should govern themselves; example of social contract)

text: How are changes made to the U.S. Constitution?
answer: Amendments (the amendment process)

text: What does the Bill of Rights protect?
answer: (The basic) rights of Americans (or people living in the United States)

text: How many amendments does the U.S. Constitution have?
answer: Twenty-seven (27)

text: Why is the Declaration of Independence important?
answer: It says America is free from British control (it says all people are created equal; it identifies inherent rights; it identifies individual freedoms)

text: What founding document said the American colonies were free from Britain?
answer: Declaration of Independence

text: Name two important ideas from the Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution.
answer: Equality; Liberty (social contract; natural rights; limited government; self-government)

text: The words “Life, Liberty, and the pursuit of Happiness” are in what founding document?
answer: Declaration of Independence

text: What is the economic system of the United States?
answer: Capitalism (free market economy)

text: What is the “rule of law”?
answer: Everyone must follow the law (leaders and government must obey the law; no one is above the law)

text: Many documents influenced the U.S. Constitution. Name one.
answer: Declaration of Independence (Articles of Confederation; Federalist Papers; Anti-Federalist Papers; Virginia Declaration of Rights; Fundamental Orders of Connecticut; Mayflower Compact; Iroquois Great Law of Peace)

text: There are three branches of government. Why?
answer: So one part does not become too powerful (checks and balances; separation of powers)

text: Name the three branches of government.
answer: Legislative, executive, and judicial (Congress, president, and the courts)

text: The President of the United States is in charge of which branch of government?
answer: Executive branch

text: What part of the federal government writes laws?
answer: (U.S.) Congress (national legislature; legislative branch)

text: What are the two parts of the U.S. Congress?
answer: Senate and House of Representatives

text: Name one power of the U.S. Congress.
answer: Writes laws (declares war; makes the federal budget)

text: How many U.S. Senators are there?
answer: One hundred (100)

text: How long is a term for a U.S. Senator?
answer: Six (6) years

text: Who is one of your state’s U.S. Senators now?
answer: [[senator_1_name]] (or [[senator_2_name]])

text: How many voting members are in the House of Representatives?
answer: Four hundred thirty-five (435)

text: How long is a term for a member of the House of Representatives?
answer: Two (2) years

text: Why do U.S. Representatives serve shorter terms than U.S. Senators?
answer: To more closely follow public opinion

text: How many Senators does each state have?
answer: Two (2)

text: Why does each state have two Senators?
answer: Equal representation for small states (The Great Compromise)

text: Name your U.S. Representative.
answer: Check uscis.gov for your local representative

text: What is the name of the Speaker of the House of Representatives now?
answer: [[speaker_house_name]]

text: Who does a U.S. Senator represent?
answer: Citizens of their state (people of their state)

text: Who elects U.S. Senators?
answer: Citizens from their state

text: Who does a member of the House of Representatives represent?
answer: Citizens in their district (people in their district)

text: Who elects members of the House of Representatives?
answer: Citizens from their district

text: Some states have more Representatives than other states. Why?
answer: Because of the state’s population (they have more people)

text: The President of the United States is elected for how many years?
answer: Four (4) years

text: The President of the United States can serve only two terms. Why?
answer: Because of the 22nd Amendment (to keep the president from becoming too powerful)

text: What is the name of the President of the United States now?
answer: [[president_name]]

text: What is the name of the Vice President of the United States now?
answer: [[vp_name]]

text: If the President can no longer serve, who becomes President?
answer: The Vice President (of the United States)

text: Name one power of the President.
answer: Signs bills into law (vetoes bills; enforces laws; Commander in Chief; appoints federal judges)

text: Who is Commander in Chief of the U.S. military?
answer: The President (of the United States)

text: Who signs bills to become laws?
answer: The President (of the United States)

text: Who vetoes bills?
answer: The President (of the United States)

text: Who appoints federal judges?
answer: The President (of the United States)

text: The Executive Branch has many parts. Name one.
answer: President (Cabinet; federal departments and agencies)

text: What does the President’s Cabinet do?
answer: Advises the President (of the United States)

text: What are two Cabinet-level positions?
answer: Attorney General; Secretary of State (Agriculture, Commerce, Education, Energy, etc.)

text: Why is the Electoral College important?
answer: It decides who is elected president

text: What is one part of the Judicial Branch?
answer: Supreme Court (federal courts)

text: What does the Judicial Branch do?
answer: Reviews laws (explains laws; resolves disputes; decides if a law goes against the Constitution)

text: What is the highest court in the United States?
answer: Supreme Court

text: How many seats are on the Supreme Court?
answer: Nine (9)

text: How many Supreme Court justices are usually needed to decide a case?
answer: Five (5)

text: How long do Supreme Court justices serve?
answer: For life (lifetime appointment; until retirement)

text: Supreme Court justices serve for life. Why?
answer: To be independent of politics (to limit outside political influence)

text: Who is the Chief Justice of the United States now?
answer: [[chief_justice_name]]

text: Name one power that is only for the federal government.
answer: Print paper money (mint coins; declare war; create an army; make treaties; set foreign policy)

text: Name one power that is only for the states.
answer: Provide schooling; provide protection (police); provide safety; give a driver’s license; approve zoning

text: What is the purpose of the 10th Amendment?
answer: States that the powers not given to the federal government belong to the states or to the people

text: Who is the Governor of your state now?
answer: [[governor_name]]

text: What is the capital of your state?
answer: [[state_capital]]

text: There are four amendments to the U.S. Constitution about who can vote. Describe one of them.
answer: Citizens 18 and older can vote (no poll tax; any citizen can vote; male citizen of any race can vote)

text: Who can vote in federal elections, run for federal office, and serve on a jury in the United States?
answer: Citizens (U.S. citizens)

text: What are three rights of everyone living in the United States?
answer: Freedom of speech; Freedom of religion; Right to bear arms (expression, assembly, petition)

text: What do we show loyalty to when we say the Pledge of Allegiance?
answer: The United States (the flag)

text: Name two promises that new citizens make in the Oath of Allegiance.
answer: Defend the Constitution; Be loyal to the United States (obey laws, serve in military/nation)

text: How can people become United States citizens?
answer: Naturalize; be born in the United States (derive citizenship)

text: What are two examples of civic participation in the United States?
answer: Vote; run for office (join political party, help with campaign, contact officials, etc.)

text: What is one way Americans can serve their country?
answer: Vote; pay taxes (obey the law; serve in military; run for office; work for government)

text: Why is it important to pay federal taxes?
answer: Required by law (all people pay to fund the federal government; civic duty)

text: It is important for all men age 18 through 25 to register for the Selective Service. Name one reason why.
answer: Required by law (civic duty; makes the draft fair)

text: The colonists came to America for many reasons. Name one.
answer: Freedom (political liberty; religious freedom; economic opportunity; escape persecution)

text: Who lived in America before the Europeans arrived?
answer: American Indians (Native Americans)

text: What group of people was taken and sold as slaves?
answer: Africans (people from Africa)

text: What war did the Americans fight to win independence from Britain?
answer: American Revolution (Revolutionary War; War for American Independence)

text: Name one reason why the Americans declared independence from Britain.
answer: High taxes (taxation without representation; soldiers stayed in houses; no self-government)

text: Who wrote the Declaration of Independence?
answer: (Thomas) Jefferson

text: When was the Declaration of Independence adopted?
answer: July 4, 1776

text: The American Revolution had many important events. Name one.
answer: Declaration of Independence (Battle of Bunker Hill; Washington Crossing the Delaware)

text: There were 13 original states. Name five.
answer: NH, MA, RI, CT, NY, NJ, PA, DE, MD, VA, NC, SC, GA

text: What founding document was written in 1787?
answer: (U.S.) Constitution

text: The Federalist Papers supported the passage of the U.S. Constitution. Name one of the writers.
answer: James Madison (Alexander Hamilton; John Jay; Publius)

text: Why were the Federalist Papers important?
answer: They helped people understand the U.S. Constitution (they supported passing it)

text: Benjamin Franklin is famous for many things. Name one.
answer: Inventor; Diplomat (founded first free public libraries; first Postmaster General)

text: George Washington is famous for many things. Name one.
answer: “Father of Our Country” (first President; Commander in Chief of Continental Army)

text: Who is called the “Father of Our Country”?
answer: George Washington

text: Who was the first President of the United States?
answer: George Washington

text: What territory did the United States buy from France in 1803?
answer: Louisiana (Louisiana Territory)

text: Name one war fought by the United States in the 1800s.
answer: Civil War (War of 1812; Mexican-American War; Spanish-American War)

text: Name the U.S. war between the North and the South.
answer: The Civil War (The War between the States)

text: Name one problem that led to the Civil War.
answer: Slavery (economic reasons; states’ rights)

text: Abraham Lincoln is famous for many things. Name one.
answer: Helped end slavery (led the U.S. during the Civil War; saved the Union)

text: What did the Emancipation Proclamation do?
answer: Freed slaves in the Confederacy (Confederate states; most Southern states)

text: What did Susan B. Anthony do?
answer: Fought for women’s rights (civil rights)

text: Name one war fought by the United States in the 1900s.
answer: World War II (WWI; Korean War; Vietnam War; Persian Gulf War)

text: Why did the United States enter World War I?
answer: To make the world safe for democracy (German aggression; submarine warfare)

text: Who was President during World War I?
answer: (Woodrow) Wilson

text: Who was President during the Great Depression and World War II?
answer: (Franklin) Roosevelt

text: Why did the United States enter World War II?
answer: Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor (Germany attacked shipping; to stop Japanese aggression)

text: Dwight D. Eisenhower is famous for many things. Name one.
answer: General during World War II (President during 1950s; Interstate Highway System)

text: Who was the main rival of the United States during the Cold War?
answer: Soviet Union (USSR; Russia)

text: What was the main concern of the United States during the Cold War?
answer: Communism (nuclear war)

text: Why did the United States enter the Korean War?
answer: To stop the spread of communism

text: Why did the United States enter the Vietnam War?
answer: To stop the spread of communism

text: What did the civil rights movement do?
answer: Fought to end racial discrimination

text: Martin Luther King, Jr. is famous for many things. Name one.
answer: Fought for civil rights (worked for equality for all Americans)

text: What major event happened on September 11, 2001, in the United States?
answer: Terrorists attacked the United States

text: George W. Bush is famous for many things. Name one.
answer: Fought terrorism (led U.S. during 9/11 attacks; resounded to Katrina)

text: Name one of the two longest rivers in the United States.
answer: Missouri River (Mississippi River)

text: What ocean is on the West Coast of the United States?
answer: Pacific Ocean

text: What ocean is on the East Coast of the United States?
answer: Atlantic Ocean

text: Name one U.S. territory.
answer: Puerto Rico (U.S. Virgin Islands; American Samoa; Guam; Northern Mariana Islands)

text: Name one state that borders Canada.
answer: Maine; NH; VT; NY; PA; OH; MI; MN; ND; MT; ID; WA; AK

text: Name one state that borders Mexico.
answer: California; Arizona; New Mexico; Texas

text: Where is the Statue of Liberty?
answer: New York Harbor (Liberty Island)

text: Why does the flag have 13 stripes?
answer: Because there were 13 original colonies (represent original colonies)

text: Why does the flag have 50 stars?
answer: One star for each state (50 states)

text: What is the name of the national anthem?
answer: The Star-Spangled Banner

text: When is Independence Day?
answer: July 4

text: Name three national U.S. holidays.
answer: New Year’s Day; Independence Day; Christmas Day

text: What is the purpose of Memorial Day?
answer: To honor soldiers who died in military service

text: What is the purpose of Veterans Day?
answer: To honor soldiers who served in the U.S. military

text: Who was the first Secretary of State?
answer: (Thomas) Jefferson

text: Who was the first Secretary of the Treasury?
answer: (Alexander) Hamilton

text: Who was the first Attorney General?
answer: (Edmund) Randolph

text: Who was the first Postmaster General?
answer: (Benjamin) Franklin

text: Who wrote the Pledge of Allegiance?
answer: Francis Bellamy
